champion.narrative: No champion identified. The contact role list has one entry: Ooi Soon Hin (Decision Maker, Head Regional Sourcing and Digitalization & Data Analytics – Group Procurement), who is the RFP process owner but has shown no evidence of advocacy, internal selling, or proactive engagement beyond the 23 June discovery meeting (Next Step). The Next Step records no champion behavior (no introductions, no shared internal docs, no pushing back on TCS or competitors for Zycus, no agenda-driving). Sharon Ooi (Head Vendor Contracts and Compliance, attended 23 Jun meeting per Next Step) is not a contact role and has not demonstrated advocacy. The engagement is cooling: rep outreach 2 Jul (Next Step update) awaiting buyer reply, no two-way touch in 12 days per server pulse. The absence of a champion in a TCS-mediated, RFP-driven process is high-risk: if TCS has a competitor preference or the Decision Maker defers to TCS recommendations, Zycus has no insider advocate to navigate the evaluation or surface early feedback. The Next Step reference 'work with TCS and use their muscle' (2 Jul) is a rep-side strategy note, not evidence of a TCS relationship or champion. The immediate move is to ask the Decision Maker for an introduction to the TCS lead consultants driving the transformation exercise and RFP criteria, and to identify an internal coach within RHB procurement who can provide early RFP feedback, share TCS findings, and advocate for Zycus during the evaluation. Without a champion, Zycus is flying blind into an August RFP with no internal intelligence or influence path.

competition.narrative: Competition is unknown. The Salesforce Competitors field is blank, and no competitor was named in the 23 June discovery meeting or subsequent Next Step updates (23 Jun, 2 Jul). The TCS procurement transformation exercise (Next Step 2 Jul, completion end-July) may include a competitive landscape scan or vendor shortlist recommendation, but Zycus has not accessed TCS findings or learned whether TCS has an incumbent or preferred-vendor relationship. The RFP process (August release per Next Step 2 Jul) will be multi-vendor by definition, but the number of invited vendors, whether an incumbent exists, and TCS's competitive leanings are unknown. The 9-product S2P suite scope (iContract, iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, eInvoicing, iSaaS, iRisk, Merlin Intake, Agentic AI per Products field) suggests Zycus will face horizontal S2P competitors (SAP Ariba, Coupa, GEP, Ivalua, Jaggaer) and potentially point-solution AI competitors or procurement-consulting firms with software arms. The absence of competitive intelligence 12 days after discovery, with an August RFP looming, is a blind spot. The immediate move is to ask the Decision Maker directly: 'Who else are you evaluating or considering for this transformation?' and 'Does TCS have a preferred vendor recommendation or incumbent relationship we should be aware of?' The answer will determine whether Zycus is competing against an entrenched incumbent (requiring a displacement wedge), a TCS-preferred vendor (requiring TCS engagement and criteria influence), or entering a clean RFP (requiring best-practice RFP positioning and multi-thread build).

identify_pain.narrative: Procurement transformation pain is confirmed but not quantified. The Next Step states the buyer is 'currently undergoing procurement transformation exercise - by TCS. completion by end of July' (2 Jul update), indicating dissatisfaction with the current procurement operating model and a mandate for change. The 9-product S2P suite scope (iContract, iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, eInvoicing, iSaaS, iRisk, Merlin Intake, Agentic AI per Products field) implies pain across contract management, sourcing, supplier onboarding, P2P processes, invoice handling, risk monitoring, intake automation, and AI-driven efficiency, but the specific pain points (manual effort, vendor fragmentation, compliance exposure, cycle-time bottlenecks, cost leakage) were not articulated in the 23 June discovery meeting with Sharon Ooi and Ooi Soon Hin (Next Step). The pain owner is Ooi Soon Hin (Decision Maker, Head Regional Sourcing and Digitalization & Data Analytics – Group Procurement per contact role), who is accountable for procurement digitization and operational efficiency. The TCS transformation exercise is the forcing function: TCS findings (due end-July) will quantify the current-state pain and define the target operating model, but Zycus has not yet accessed those findings or co-shaped the pain narrative with the buyer. Without quantified pain, the business case for a $250k investment is generic. The immediate move is to request a follow-up call with the Decision Maker to surface and quantify current-state pain (e.g. manual contract redlining cost, sourcing cycle time, supplier onboarding delays, invoice error rate, compliance risk exposure) and align Zycus's solution narrative with the TCS transformation findings before the RFP release in August.

paper_process.narrative: Paper process (MSA/SOW, legal review, procurement channel, InfoSec) is unknown. The Next Step references Sharon Ooi as Head Vendor Contracts and Compliance (23 Jun meeting), indicating legal/contracting will be her domain, but no discussion of MSA, SOW, redline timeline, legal approval gates, or InfoSec review has been recorded. The 148-day close window (30 Nov 2026) with RFP not releasing until August leaves ~120 days for post-RFP vendor evaluation AND all contracting steps, which is aggressive for a banking-group procurement with compliance and InfoSec requirements. No SI or partner involvement has been mentioned. The TCS procurement transformation exercise (Next Step 2 Jul) may define a new procurement channel or vendor onboarding process, but Zycus has not accessed those findings. The Products field includes iSaaS, indicating a SaaS delivery model that will trigger InfoSec, data-residency, and ERP/systems-integration reviews, but none of these have been scoped. The immediate move is to ask Sharon Ooi (Vendor Contracts/Compliance contact from 23 Jun meeting) for the contracting timeline, MSA/SOW template, legal review SLA, and InfoSec requirements, and to request early legal and InfoSec kickoff during the RFP evaluation phase (not after vendor selection) to de-risk the close timeline.

decision_process.narrative: The decision process is RFP-driven and TCS-mediated. The Next Step timeline shows: (1) TCS procurement transformation exercise underway, completion end-July 2026; (2) RFP release August 2026 (delayed from end-July per Next Step 2 Jul update); (3) vendor evaluation post-RFP; (4) close 30 Nov 2026, leaving ~120 days for evaluation, shortlist, commercials, legal, InfoSec, and contracting. The Discovery meeting on 23 June (Next Step) involved Sharon Ooi (Head Vendor Contracts and Compliance) and Ooi Soon Hin (Decision Maker, Head Regional Sourcing), indicating procurement and compliance are co-drivers of the process, with TCS as the third-party shaper. No escalation, C-level involvement, or Economic Buyer approval step has been mapped. The buyer has not disclosed the evaluation timeline post-RFP, shortlist criteria, number of vendors, or whether there will be a demo/workshop phase before commercials. The absence of an Economic Buyer or CFO approval step in the known process is a gap that will surface during contract negotiation. The Next Step does not reference any internal approval gates, legal review timeline, or InfoSec requirements. The immediate move is to ask the Decision Maker for the full end-to-end process map (RFP submission deadline, evaluation phases, shortlist notification, demo/workshop, commercials, approvals, legal, InfoSec, contracting milestones) to plan Zycus's resourcing and identify when to engage the Economic Buyer and other approval-chain stakeholders.

decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria are being defined by TCS as part of their procurement transformation exercise (due end-July per Next Step 2 Jul), but Zycus has not yet accessed the criteria or influenced their shape. The Next Step states 'RFP will be released in Aug 26, currently undergoing procurement transformation exercise - by TCS. completion by end of July', indicating TCS is defining the target operating model and will set the RFP evaluation framework. The 9-product scope (iContract, iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, eInvoicing, iSaaS, iRisk, Merlin Intake, Agentic AI per Products field) suggests the buyer's criteria will span contract lifecycle, sourcing, supplier management, P2P, invoicing, SaaS delivery, risk, intake automation, and agentic AI capabilities, but the relative weighting, must-haves, and technical requirements are unknown to the relevant stakeholder meeting on 23 June (Next Step) did not surface detailed evaluation criteria or scorecards. Without early access to the TCS findings and RFP criteria preview, Zycus risks a criteria mismatch (e.g. TCS may deprioritize AI/Merlin if they are unfamiliar with agentic procurement, or shape integration requirements around a competitor's architecture). The immediate move is to request a pre-RFP criteria preview from the Decision Maker and to engage TCS directly (as suggested in Next Step 2 Jul: 'work with TCS and use their muscle') to understand and influence the evaluation framework before the August RFP release.

north_star_verdict.headline: Early-stage deal cooling 12 days after discovery; TCS-driven RFP in August requires pre-RFP influence now to shape criteria, but no buyer reply since rep outreach 2 July and no verified two-way engagement since 23 June meeting.

flags: Influence the consultant before the RFP locks. TCS is defining the target operating model and RFP scorecard (completion end-July 2026), which will shape evaluation criteria and may embed competitor preferences or requirements Zycus cannot meet. Early engagement with TCS and the Decision Maker during July to share Zycus S2P+Merlin capabilities, banking references, and AI differentiation can shape the RFP in Zycus favor. Without this, Zycus may be responding to a consultant-designed RFP optimized for another vendor. Recommend Deal team coordinate a TCS briefing or three-way session (Zycus-RHB-TCS) in early July 2026 to position Merlin AI and S2P breadth as transformation enablers before criteria finalize.

Map the Economic Buyer and budget approval chain now, before RFP. At $250k in a Tier-1 regulated bank, commercial sign-off will escalate above the Decision Maker (Ooi Soon Hin, VP Sourcing). Likely EB candidates: Group CFO, Group Chief Procurement Officer, or C-level transformation sponsor (the TCS engagement suggests Board or exec sponsorship). Pricing, terms, and contract negotiation will stall post-RFP if the EB is unmapped and unsold. Recommended move: ask the Decision Maker during next engagement (early July) who will approve the budget and sign the contract, and request an intro or briefing so Zycus can align commercial proposal and ROI story to EB priorities before RFP response.

Expand the stakeholder map and build a champion or coach. The deal is single-threaded (1 buyer contact role, 3 meeting attendees) with no champion. Sharon Ooi (compliance lead) and Lee Wan Teen (role unknown) attended the 2026-06-23 meeting but are not added as contact roles, and neither has shown coaching behavior. In a consultant-mediated RFP, insider guidance on TCS priorities, RFP scoring weights, and internal politics is critical to win. Recommended move: add Sharon Ooi and Lee Wan Teen as contact roles, map their influence and access to EB, and assess during July engagement whether any attendee or a new stakeholder (e.g., line-of-business procurement lead, IT integration sponsor) will coach Zycus on how to win the RFP and navigate RHB decision dynamics.

Lee Wan Teen

Influence the consultant before the RFP locks. TCS is defining the target operating model and RFP scorecard (completion end-July 2026), which will shape evaluation criteria and may embed competitor preferences or requirements Zycus cannot meet. Early engagement with TCS and the Decision Maker during July to share Zycus S2P+Merlin capabilities, banking references, and AI differentiation can shape the RFP in Zycus favor. Without this, Zycus may be responding to a consultant-designed RFP optimized for another vendor. Recommend Deal team coordinate a TCS briefing or three-way session (Zycus-RHB-TCS) in early July 2026 to position Merlin AI and S2P breadth as transformation enablers before criteria finalize.

Map the Economic Buyer and budget approval chain now, before RFP. At $250k in a Tier-1 regulated bank, commercial sign-off will escalate above the Decision Maker (Ooi Soon Hin, VP Sourcing). Likely EB candidates: Group CFO, Group Chief Procurement Officer, or C-level transformation sponsor (the TCS engagement suggests Board or exec sponsorship). Pricing, terms, and contract negotiation will stall post-RFP if the EB is unmapped and unsold. Recommended move: ask the Decision Maker during next engagement (early July) who will approve the budget and sign the contract, and request an intro or briefing so Zycus can align commercial proposal and ROI story to EB priorities before RFP response.

Expand the stakeholder map and build a champion or coach. The deal is single-threaded (1 buyer contact role, 3 meeting attendees) with no champion. Sharon Ooi (compliance lead) and Lee Wan Teen (role unknown) attended the 2026-06-23 meeting but are not added as contact roles, and neither has shown coaching behavior. In a consultant-mediated RFP, insider guidance on TCS priorities, RFP scoring weights, and internal politics is critical to win. Recommended move: add Sharon Ooi and Lee Wan Teen as contact roles, map their influence and access to EB, and assess during July engagement whether any attendee or a new stakeholder (e.g., line-of-business procurement lead, IT integration sponsor) will coach Zycus on how to win the RFP and navigate RHB decision dynamics.

Recommend Deal team coordinate a TCS briefing or three-way session (Zycus-RHB-TCS) in early July 2026 to position Merlin AI and S2P breadth as transformation enablers before criteria finalize.
